message.content: are there tools that take peoples handwriting and turn it into typed text

content.text:
Yes, there are several good options:

**Cloud APIs:**
- **Google Cloud Vision API** — Strong handwriting OCR (HTR), supports many languages
- **AWS Textract** — Handwriting recognition built in, works well for forms and documents
- **Azure AI Vision (formerly Computer Vision)** — Microsoft's OCR with handwriting support
- **Google ML Kit** — Free on-device handwriting recognition for mobile apps

**Open Source / Self-hosted:**
- **Tesseract OCR** — Free, widely used; handwriting support is limited compared to cloud options
- **TrOCR (Microsoft)** — Transformer-based model specifically for handwriting recognition, available via Hugging Face
- **EasyOCR** — Python library, supports handwriting to some extent
- **Kraken** — Focused on historical/handwritten document recognition

**On-device / Real-time:**
- **Apple Vision framework** — `VNRecognizeTextRequest` handles handwriting on macOS/iOS
- **MyScript** — Commercial SDK for real-time handwriting recognition (stylus input)

For most projects, **Google Cloud Vision** or **TrOCR** tend to give the best results on handwritten text. If you're building something in this repo, want me to look at what you have so far and suggest which would fit best?

message.content: are there any that are like mediapipe cdn but for handwriting

content.text:
**Tesseract.js** is the closest match — it's a full OCR engine that runs in the browser via WebAssembly, loadable from a CDN:

```html
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/tesseract.js@5/dist/tesseract.min.js"></script>
```

```js
const worker = await Tesseract.createWorker('eng');
const { data: { text } } = await worker.recognize(imageOrCanvas);
console.log(text);
```

**Caveats:** Tesseract.js is decent for printed text but its handwriting recognition is mediocre. There's no true "MediaPipe-quality" browser-native handwriting recognizer yet.

**Other browser-friendly options:**

- **TensorFlow.js** — You can load a handwriting model (like one trained on IAM dataset) and run inference client-side, but you'd need to find/train the model yourself
- **ONNX Runtime Web** — Same idea, lets you run models like TrOCR in-browser via WebAssembly/WebGL, but heavier
- **HandwritingRecognition API** (Chrome origin trial) — A proposed browser API (`navigator.createHandwritingRecognizer()`) for real-time stylus/touch input recognition. Chrome-only and experimental.

If you want the simplest CDN drop-in, **Tesseract.js** is the way to go. Want me to look at your project and wire it up?

content.text:
Done. Open it with:

```
open index.html
```

**What it does:**
- **Draw tab** — write on a canvas with your mouse/trackpad/stylus
- **Upload tab** — drag & drop or click to upload a photo of handwriting
- Hit **Recognize** and TrOCR runs entirely in-browser

**Heads up:** The first time you hit Recognize, it downloads the model (~65MB). After that it's cached by the browser. 

Note that TrOCR works best on **single lines of handwriting**. If you have multiple lines, accuracy drops. If that becomes an issue we could add line segmentation.
Let me check the current state first.

content.text:
Run this from the project directory:

```

```

Then open the URL it gives you (usually `http://localhost:3000`). You need a local server because the Transformers.js import won't work from a `file://` URL.

I found comprehensive information about browser-based handwriting recognition using TrOCR and Transformers.js. Here's what the search revealed:

## TrOCR Overview

TrOCR was introduced in the paper "TrOCR: Transformer-based Optical Character Recognition with Pre-trained Models" by Li et al. The TrOCR model is an encoder-decoder model, consisting of an image Transformer as encoder, and a text Transformer as decoder. The image encoder was initialized from the weights of BEiT, while the text decoder was initialized from the weights of RoBERTa.

## Browser-Based Implementation with Transformers.js

Transformers.js uses ONNX Runtime to run models in the browser. For handwriting recognition specifically, the Xenova/trocr-base-handwritten model provides ONNX weights to be compatible with Transformers.js.

## Example Usage

The Transformers.js JavaScript library can be installed from NPM using: npm i @xenova/transformers Here's how to use it: You can create an image-to-text pipeline with "pipeline('image-to-text', 'Xenova/trocr-base-handwritten')" and perform optical character recognition on handwriting images.

## Open Source Projects

The search found several open source implementations:
- An unofficial implementation of the paper "TrOCR: Transformer-based Optical Character Recognition with Pre-trained Models" on GitHub
- A web application designed for Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) that allows users to upload handwritten image files and receive digitized text outputs, with features like dynamic text highlighting and a user-friendly interface.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, I found several open-source GitHub projects related to handwriting-to-text or text-to-handwriting web applications. Here are some notable projects:

## Text-to-Handwriting Projects (Typed Text → Handwritten Output)

There is a privacy-first web app featuring procedural rendering, organic jitter simulation, and intelligent pagination, updated in February 2026, built with React, TypeScript, and other modern tools.

Some specific projects include:

1. **handwritten.js** - Supports output types of pdf, jpeg/buf, jpeg/b64, png/buf and png/b64, returning a promise that resolves in a pdfkit document instance or an array containing the buffer or base64 value.

2. **handwriter** - Recognizes text from image files (OCR) for more than 100 languages using tesseract.js, converts text to handwriting with more than 10 fonts, and can export output to image or combine output images to pdf.

3. **Type-To-Write** - A simple web app that converts typed text into natural handwriting.

4. **TypeScribe** - Uses Machine Learning to convert typed text into realistic handwriting by introducing customizable degrees of randomness and variations, with 12 predefined handwriting styles and options to adjust line spacing, page size, margins, ink color, and pen thickness.

## Handwriting-to-Text Projects (Handwritten Input → Digital Text)

There's "InkFlow AI Convert," an application that offers a seamless solution for turning handwritten text into digital format.

Most of these projects are available on GitHub and are open-source. You can find them by searching for "text-to-handwriting" or "handwriting-ocr" on GitHub.

Here are the search results for handwriting recognition to digital text with JavaScript, browser canvas, and OCR on GitHub:

## Key Projects and Libraries

**handwriting.js** is an open-sourced library that provides access to Google's handwriting recognition service and implements canvas behaviors for capturing handwriting, sending data in proper format, and providing undo/redo functionality.

There's a demonstration of open-source handwriting recognition in a webVR environment, powered by Tensorflow.js, OpenCV.js, A-Frame and Three.js.

**ocrad.js** provides OCR in Javascript via Emscripten.

**Tesseract.js** is a javascript version of the Tesseract Open Source OCR Engine used for Optical Character Recognition.

## Canvas-Based Solutions

There are parameterizable canvas libraries that allow you to draw on canvas and get the drawn points.

Using TensorFlow.js with canvas, developers have created webapps that can predict letters and numbers by feeding bounding box images from the canvas to a classifier model.

## Recognition Approaches

The search results show several GitHub repositories for handwriting OCR, including:
- OCR software for recognition of handwritten text from photos, with support for multiple languages like Czech.
- Web apps to convert handwritten forms to digital forms, where users upload handwritten forms which are converted to digital text.

There's a framework-agnostic handwritten math expression recognition running entirely in the browser, powered by ONNX inference engine.

You can explore more projects by visiting the GitHub topics pages for handwriting-recognition and handwriting-ocr, which aggregate numerous open-source projects in this area.

content.text:
Here are the most relevant projects:

**Handwriting → Text (what you're building):**

- [**handwriting.js**](https://www.chenyuho.com/project/handwritingjs/) — Uses Google's handwriting recognition API with a canvas. Draw on canvas, it sends stroke data to Google and returns text. Very accurate since it uses Google's service.
- [**Breta01/handwriting-ocr**](https://github.com/Breta01/handwriting-ocr) — OCR for handwritten text from photos
- [**LongIsHandsome/trocr-webapp**](https://github.com/LongIsHandsome/trocr-webapp) — Web app using TrOCR specifically for handwriting recognition
- [**sarveshh/handwriter**](https://github.com/sarveshh/handwriter) — Uses Tesseract.js for OCR + converts text to handwriting fonts

**Text → Handwriting (opposite direction, but popular):**

- [**saurabhdaware/text-to-handwriting**](https://github.com/saurabhdaware/text-to-handwriting) — Converts typed text into handwriting images (16k+ stars)
- [**alias-rahil/handwritten.js**](https://github.com/alias-rahil/handwritten.js) — Typed text to realistic handwriting output

The **handwriting.js** approach is interesting — instead of running a local model, it sends the actual stroke data (not an image) to Google's recognizer, which is why it's much more accurate. Want me to look into swapping our approach to something like that?

Sources:
- [handwriting.js](https://www.chenyuho.com/project/handwritingjs/)
- [handwriting-ocr](https://github.com/Breta01/handwriting-ocr)
- [trocr-webapp](https://github.com/LongIsHandsome/trocr-webapp)
- [handwriting-recognition GitHub topic](https://github.com/topics/handwriting-recognition?l=javascript)
- [handwriter](https://github.com/sarveshh/handwriter)

message.content: the only thing is i dont want to use an api key or pay

content.text:
That narrows it down. Your best free, no-API-key options for better accuracy:

1. **Auto-crop + TrOCR base model** — Stay with our current approach but crop the canvas to just the ink and use the larger `trocr-base-handwritten` model. Free, fully client-side, no API key. Should noticeably improve results.

2. **Google's handwriting API (no key needed)** — handwriting.js sends stroke data to `inputtools.google.com`, which is a free public endpoint (same one Google Input Tools uses). No API key required. Very accurate because it gets stroke order, not just a static image.

Option 2 would be the biggest accuracy jump since stroke-based recognition beats image-based for canvas drawing. Want me to try that approach?

Based on the search results, here's what I found about handwriting.js, Google's input tools API, and canvas stroke recognition with JavaScript:

## handwriting.js Overview

handwriting.js, an open-sourced library released on GitHub, provides access to Google's handwriting recognition service. Since there's no official documents or APIs for this service, this library also implements a set of canvas behaviors which can capture, send handwritings in proper format and has undo/redo functionality.

## Stroke Recognition Data Format

The trace is an array of strokes, where a stroke is an array of pairs of captured (x, y) coordinates - each pair of (x, y) coordinates represents one sampling point. For example, a trace composed of two strokes could be represented as: (300, 320), (310, 320), (320, 320), (330, 320), (340, 320) and another stroke with different coordinates.

## Canvas Integration Example

You can include handwriting.canvas.js instead of handwriting.js if you want to use the pre-implemented canvas behaviors. You declare an HTML canvas as a handwriting.Canvas object by passing that canvas element to the constructor.

## Recognition Results

The recognition function will invoke the callback function argument passed in after recognition process. The callback function should accept two arguments, and the returned result will be an array of string.

For more detailed implementation examples and documentation, you can visit the official GitHub repository at https://github.com/ChenYuHo/handwriting.js/

## handwriting.js Overview

handwriting.js, an open-sourced library released on GitHub, provides access to Google's handwriting recognition service. Since there's no official documents or APIs for this service, this library also implements a set of canvas behaviors which can capture, send handwritings in proper format and has undo/redo functionality.

## Stroke Recognition Data Format

The trace is an array of strokes, where a stroke is an array of pairs of captured (x, y) coordinates - each pair of (x, y) coordinates represents one sampling point. For example, a trace composed of two strokes could be represented as: (300, 320), (310, 320), (320, 320), (330, 320), (340, 320) and another stroke with different coordinates.

## Canvas Integration Example

You can include handwriting.canvas.js instead of handwriting.js if you want to use the pre-implemented canvas behaviors. You declare an HTML canvas as a handwriting.Canvas object by passing that canvas element to the constructor.

## Recognition Results

The recognition function will invoke the callback function argument passed in after recognition process. The callback function should accept two arguments, and the returned result will be an array of string.

For more detailed implementation examples and documentation, you can visit the official GitHub repository at https://github.com/ChenYuHo/handwriting.js/

content.text:
Here's what changed:

- **Stroke-based recognition** — captures your actual pen strokes (x, y, time) and sends them to Google's free handwriting service, which is way more accurate than image-based OCR
- **Auto-recognize** — starts recognizing 1 second after you stop drawing (no button needed)
- **Undo** — remove last stroke
- **Alternative matches** — shows other possible words in the status bar
- **No model download** — instant, no 65MB wait
- **Language set to English** (`en` instead of the default `zh_TW`)

Try it out with `npx serve .` — should be a big improvement over TrOCR. One caveat: the Google endpoint might block requests from `localhost` due to CORS. If that happens, let me know and I'll add a workaround.
No, I didn't — you only asked me to try it out last time. Want me to commit and push now?

of static images. Adds auto-recognize, undo, and alternative matches.
No API key or model download required.
